policy.text:
April 2025 New York State Medicaid Update pg. 7

Pharmacy Reminder: Coordination of Benefits Processing
Instruction for New York State Medicaid
New York State (NYS) Medicaid providers are required to bill applicable third parties that may be liable for a
claim before billing NYS Medicaid. NYS Medicaid is always the payer of last resort. Federal regulations require
that all other available resources be used before NYS Medicaid considers payment. Through coordination of
benefits (COB), NYS Medicaid will pay the patient responsibility for correctly submitted NYS Medicaid
coverable claims, up to the NYS Medicaid allowed amount.
Providers must exhaust all existing benefits prior to billing the NYS Medicaid. Providers should always ask a
NYS Medicaid member if they have other third-party coverage to ensure the proper COB. Pharmacies are
responsible for submitting claims to other coverage before submitting claims to NYS Medicaid. Pharmacies
are required to coordinate with the proper primary payer. This includes submitting a claim under the
appropriate coverage benefit, medical or pharmacy.
policy.title: New York State Medicaid Update April 2025 Volume 41 Number 4
policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

policy.text:
6

Coordination of Benefits (COB) ensures the correct party pays first. NYS Medicaid is always the payor of
last resort; federal regulations require that all other available resources be used before NYS Medicaid
considers payment. If there is a responsible third-party that should be paying for the health benefits of the
patient such as a health insurance provider, the responsible third-party should pay first. NYS Medicaid pays
the lesser of Patient Responsibility (PR) or the NYS Medicaid fee, regardless of the PR amount. For
pharmacies, this rule applies to all PR, which includes deductible, co-insurance, copay, and other patient
responsibility.
Effective November 1, 2022, NYS Medicaid will not accept a combination of Other Coverage Code of
"3" in National Council for Prescription Drug Program (NCPDP) field 308-C8 (Other Coverage Code)
with any reject code in field 472-6E when another third party is responsible for payment. The pre-
adjudication edit will be expanded for this field and will return the NCPDP Reject Code (DE 3988) (6E - M/I
Other Payer Reject Code).
The provider must work with the primary insurance to obtain coverage for the member.

Update to New York State Medicaid Fee-for-Service Pharmacy Billing
Instructions for Coordination of Benefits Submission
NYRx, the New York State (NYS) Medicaid Pharmacy program, needs to ensure accurate Coordination of
Benefits (COB) submissions are recognized and other patient responsibility amounts are accepted. This is an
update to previous guidance issued in the Update on Medicaid Fee-for-Service (FFS) Pharmacy Billing
Instructions for Coordination of Benefits (COB) Submission article published in the July 2017 issue of the
Medicaid Update, located at: https://www.health.ny.gov/health_care/medicaid/program/update/2017/jul17_
mu.pdf.
Federal regulations require that all other available resources be used before Medicaid considers payment. If
there is a responsible third-party that should be paying for the patients' health benefits, such as a health
insurance provider, the responsible third-party should pay first. NYS Medicaid pays the lesser of Patient
Responsibility (PR) or the NYS Medicaid fee, regardless of the PR amount.

provider_manual.text:
• Maintain inventory of commonly dispensed medications and supplies (e.g., diabetic), that are applicable to
the patients they serve.
• Maintain adequate hours of operation, as needed for the Medicaid members that they serve.
• Are able to respond to urgent or emergent issues that occur after normal operating hours.
• Out of state pharmacies:
o must maintain NY non-resident registration.
o must maintain state registration in service location.
o must maintain Pharmacy / Wholesale Distributor / Manufacturer License Verification with the NYS
Education Department.
o must maintain Medicaid enrollment in service location state.
• Update the Department as required for pharmacy enrollment changes as per the General Policy Guidelines.

regulations.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
Workers’ Compensation and Coordination of Benefits
If a member has a work-related injury, for which they have an open Workers’ Compensation case, Workers’
compensation coverage must be utilized prior to billing the NYRx Medicaid Pharmacy Program.
If the drug claim is NOT related to a member’s Workers’ Compensation case, such as a maintenance medication for an
unrelated chronic condition, NYRx may be billed as the primary payer with an Eligibility Clarification Code of “2” in field
309-C9. Medicaid is the payer of last resort, and providers must ensure all other responsible payers are included on the
claim, if applicable.
The Workers’ Compensation Board can be accessed here: https://www.wcb.ny.gov/content/main/hcpp/hcpp.jsp.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
Note:
• Outpatient dispensing, community-based, open-door pharmacies applying for enrollment in COS 0441
must be full service. This is defined as dispensing and maintaining an inventory of commonly dispensed
outpatient prescription medications, as well as providing diabetic supplies and immunizations. Community
pharmacies that serve a particular diagnosis, situation, or niche or that do not immunize do not meet COS
0441 enrollment criteria.
• Pharmacies that are embedded or located (not separate and distinct) within a healthcare facility, physician’s
office, or group, or in a hospital setting, are not classified or enrollable under COS 0441 (see clinic and
hospital pharmacy COS below), this includes pharmacies with pass through windows or doors, or situated
where patients have to walk through a healthcare facility, office area, or otherwise to reach the pharmacy or
those that share a waiting area.
• Pharmacies whose physical registered space is not separate and distinct from other unaffiliated or
separately operated retail or medical entities do not qualify for pharmacy enrollment.

provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

answers.headline_summary: New York State Medicaid requires providers to bill all applicable third parties before billing Medicaid, as Medicaid is always the payer of last resort. For work-related injuries, Workers' Compensation coverage must be utilized first, unless the claim is for an unrelated chronic condition.

aligned_pairs.provider_manual: Medicaid functions as the payer of last resort, necessitating that providers include all other responsible payers on a claim. For work-related injuries with an open Workers' Compensation case, providers must utilize Workers' Compensation coverage before billing the NYRx Medicaid Pharmacy Program. If a drug claim is unrelated to a Workers' Compensation case, NYRx can be billed as the primary payer using an Eligibility Clarification Code of '2'.
aligned_pairs.policy_update: New York State Medicaid providers are mandated to bill all applicable third parties liable for a claim before submitting it to NYS Medicaid, as Medicaid consistently serves as the payer of last resort. Federal regulations stipulate that all other available resources must be exhausted first. Providers are required to exhaust all existing benefits and inquire with members about any other third-party coverage they may possess.

similarities: Both the provider manual and policy update emphasize that Medicaid is always the payer of last resort [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines, New York State Medicaid Update April 2025 Volume 41 Number 4].

Both sources require providers to bill all other applicable third parties before billing New York State Medicaid [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines, New York State Medicaid Update April 2025 Volume 41 Number 4].
differences: The provider manual specifically details the process for Workers' Compensation claims, including when NYRx may be billed as primary for unrelated conditions with an Eligibility Clarification Code of '2' [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines].

The policy update broadly states that providers must exhaust all existing benefits and ask members about other third-party coverage, without specifying particular third-party payers like Workers' Compensation [New York State Medicaid Update April 2025 Volume 41 Number 4].

aligned_pairs.provider_manual: Medicaid functions as the payer of last resort, necessitating that providers include all other responsible payers on a claim if applicable. For work-related injuries with an open Workers' Compensation case, Workers' Compensation coverage must be used before billing the NYRx Medicaid Pharmacy Program. If a drug claim is not related to a Workers' Compensation case, NYRx may be billed as the primary payer with an Eligibility Clarification Code of "2" in field 309-C9.
aligned_pairs.policy_update: New York State Medicaid is consistently the payer of last resort, and federal regulations mandate that all other available resources be exhausted before NYS Medicaid considers payment. Providers are required to exhaust all existing benefits prior to billing NYS Medicaid. Through coordination of benefits, NYS Medicaid will cover the patient responsibility for correctly submitted Medicaid-coverable claims, up to the allowed amount.

similarities: Both the provider manual and policy state that Medicaid is the payer of last resort [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines, New York State Medicaid Update April 2025 Volume 41 Number 4].

Both documents require providers to utilize all other available resources or responsible payers before billing NYS Medicaid [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines, New York State Medicaid Update April 2025 Volume 41 Number 4].
differences: The provider manual specifically addresses Workers' Compensation coverage for work-related injuries as a primary payer before billing the NYRx Medicaid Pharmacy Program, which is not detailed in the policy update [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines].

The policy update mentions that NYS Medicaid will pay the patient responsibility for correctly submitted claims up to the allowed amount, a detail not explicitly stated in the provider manual [New York State Medicaid Update April 2025 Volume 41 Number 4].

The provider manual provides a specific Eligibility Clarification Code for billing NYRx as primary payer for non-work-related claims, which is not present in the policy update [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines].

test_id_33.query_content: What are the requirements for Coordination of Benefits (COB)?

policy.text:
Additional information regarding Coordination
of Benefits (COB) can be found in the following Medicaid Update articles:
• Update on Coordination of Benefits Submissions for Medicaid Fee-for-Service Pharmacy Billing article
published in the October 2022 issue of the Medicaid Update, located at: https://
www.health.ny.gov/health_care/medicaid/program/update/2022/docs/mu_no12_oct22_pr.pdf;
• Reminder: Medicaid Requires Coordination of Benefits article published in the April 2022 issue of the
Medicaid Update, located at: https://www.health.ny.gov/health_care/medicaid/program/update/2022/
docs/mu_no4_apr22_pr.pdf;
• Reminder: Pharmacy Billing for Medicaid Patients with Other Coverage article published in the
November 2021 issue of the Medicaid Update, located at: https://www.health.ny.gov/
health_care/medicaid/program/update/2021/docs/mu_no13_nov21_pr.pdf; and
• Attention Pharmacy Providers: Coordination of Benefits (COB) Processing Change for Other Payer
ID and Other Payer ID Qualifier article published in the October 2017 issue of the Medicaid Update,
located at: https://www.health.ny.gov/health_care/medicaid/program/update/2017/oct17_mu.pdf.
policy.title: Medicaid Update Special Edition Part Four: NYRx Pharmacy Benefit Transition Medicaid Update Volume 39 Number 9
policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

policy.text:
April 2025 New York State Medicaid Update pg. 7

Pharmacy Reminder: Coordination of Benefits Processing
Instruction for New York State Medicaid
New York State (NYS) Medicaid providers are required to bill applicable third parties that may be liable for a
claim before billing NYS Medicaid. NYS Medicaid is always the payer of last resort. Federal regulations require
that all other available resources be used before NYS Medicaid considers payment. Through coordination of
benefits (COB), NYS Medicaid will pay the patient responsibility for correctly submitted NYS Medicaid
coverable claims, up to the NYS Medicaid allowed amount.
Providers must exhaust all existing benefits prior to billing the NYS Medicaid. Providers should always ask a
NYS Medicaid member if they have other third-party coverage to ensure the proper COB. Pharmacies are
responsible for submitting claims to other coverage before submitting claims to NYS Medicaid. Pharmacies
are required to coordinate with the proper primary payer. This includes submitting a claim under the
appropriate coverage benefit, medical or pharmacy.
policy.title: New York State Medicaid Update April 2025 Volume 41 Number 4
policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

policy.text:
6

Coordination of Benefits (COB) ensures the correct party pays first. NYS Medicaid is always the payor of
last resort; federal regulations require that all other available resources be used before NYS Medicaid
considers payment. If there is a responsible third-party that should be paying for the health benefits of the
patient such as a health insurance provider, the responsible third-party should pay first. NYS Medicaid pays
the lesser of Patient Responsibility (PR) or the NYS Medicaid fee, regardless of the PR amount. For
pharmacies, this rule applies to all PR, which includes deductible, co-insurance, copay, and other patient
responsibility.
Effective November 1, 2022, NYS Medicaid will not accept a combination of Other Coverage Code of
"3" in National Council for Prescription Drug Program (NCPDP) field 308-C8 (Other Coverage Code)
with any reject code in field 472-6E when another third party is responsible for payment. The pre-
adjudication edit will be expanded for this field and will return the NCPDP Reject Code (DE 3988) (6E - M/I
Other Payer Reject Code).
The provider must work with the primary insurance to obtain coverage for the member.
policy.title: New York State Medicaid Update October 2022 Volume 38 Number 12
policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

policy.text:
May 2023 New York State Medicaid Update pg. 5

Update to New York State Medicaid Fee-for-Service Pharmacy Billing
Instructions for Coordination of Benefits Submission
NYRx, the New York State (NYS) Medicaid Pharmacy program, needs to ensure accurate Coordination of
Benefits (COB) submissions are recognized and other patient responsibility amounts are accepted. This is an
update to previous guidance issued in the Update on Medicaid Fee-for-Service (FFS) Pharmacy Billing
Instructions for Coordination of Benefits (COB) Submission article published in the July 2017 issue of the
Medicaid Update, located at: https://www.health.ny.gov/health_care/medicaid/program/update/2017/jul17_
mu.pdf.
Federal regulations require that all other available resources be used before Medicaid considers payment. If
there is a responsible third-party that should be paying for the patients' health benefits, such as a health
insurance provider, the responsible third-party should pay first. NYS Medicaid pays the lesser of Patient
Responsibility (PR) or the NYS Medicaid fee, regardless of the PR amount.
policy.title: New York State Medicaid Update May 2023 Volume 39 Number 10
policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text: If all three conditions are not met, the billing provider may call the Department for assistance.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
• Maintain inventory of commonly dispensed medications and supplies (e.g., diabetic), that are applicable to
the patients they serve.
• Maintain adequate hours of operation, as needed for the Medicaid members that they serve.
• Are able to respond to urgent or emergent issues that occur after normal operating hours.
• Out of state pharmacies:
o must maintain NY non-resident registration.
o must maintain state registration in service location.
o must maintain Pharmacy / Wholesale Distributor / Manufacturer License Verification with the NYS
Education Department.
o must maintain Medicaid enrollment in service location state.
• Update the Department as required for pharmacy enrollment changes as per the General Policy Guidelines.
• Maintain all required accreditations from a CMS-approved organization in compliance with CMS
regulations.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
Workers’ Compensation and Coordination of Benefits
If a member has a work-related injury, for which they have an open Workers’ Compensation case, Workers’
compensation coverage must be utilized prior to billing the NYRx Medicaid Pharmacy Program.
If the drug claim is NOT related to a member’s Workers’ Compensation case, such as a maintenance medication for an
unrelated chronic condition, NYRx may be billed as the primary payer with an Eligibility Clarification Code of “2” in field
309-C9. Medicaid is the payer of last resort, and providers must ensure all other responsible payers are included on the
claim, if applicable.
The Workers’ Compensation Board can be accessed here: https://www.wcb.ny.gov/content/main/hcpp/hcpp.jsp.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
Note:
• Outpatient dispensing, community-based, open-door pharmacies applying for enrollment in COS 0441
must be full service. This is defined as dispensing and maintaining an inventory of commonly dispensed
outpatient prescription medications, as well as providing diabetic supplies and immunizations. Community
pharmacies that serve a particular diagnosis, situation, or niche or that do not immunize do not meet COS
0441 enrollment criteria.
• Pharmacies that are embedded or located (not separate and distinct) within a healthcare facility, physician’s
office, or group, or in a hospital setting, are not classified or enrollable under COS 0441 (see clinic and
hospital pharmacy COS below), this includes pharmacies with pass through windows or doors, or situated
where patients have to walk through a healthcare facility, office area, or otherwise to reach the pharmacy or
those that share a waiting area.
• Pharmacies whose physical registered space is not separate and distinct from other unaffiliated or
separately operated retail or medical entities do not qualify for pharmacy enrollment.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

answers.headline_summary: New York State Medicaid requires providers to bill all applicable third parties before billing Medicaid, as Medicaid is always the payer of last resort. This includes utilizing Workers' Compensation coverage for work-related injuries prior to billing the NYRx Medicaid Pharmacy Program.

aligned_pairs.provider_manual: Medicaid functions as the payer of last resort, necessitating providers to include all other responsible payers on a claim when applicable. For work-related injuries with an open Workers' Compensation case, providers must utilize Workers' Compensation coverage before billing the NYRx Medicaid Pharmacy Program. However, if a drug claim is unrelated to a Workers' Compensation case, NYRx may be billed as the primary payer using Eligibility Clarification Code "2" in field 309-C9.
aligned_pairs.policy_update: New York State Medicaid providers are mandated to bill applicable third parties who may be liable for a claim before submitting to NYS Medicaid, as NYS Medicaid consistently acts as the payer of last resort. Federal regulations stipulate that all other available resources must be exhausted before NYS Medicaid considers payment. Through coordination of benefits, NYS Medicaid will cover the patients responsibility for correctly submitted NYS Medicaid-coverable claims, up to the allowed amount.

similarities: Both the provider manual and policy update emphasize that Medicaid is the payer of last resort and requires other payers to be billed first for Coordination of Benefits [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines, New York State Medicaid Update April 2025 Volume 41 Number 4].

Both documents require providers to exhaust all existing benefits from other payers before billing New York State Medicaid [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines, New York State Medicaid Update April 2025 Volume 41 Number 4].
differences: The provider manual specifically details the process for Workers' Compensation cases, including when NYRx can be billed as the primary payer for unrelated claims, which is not explicitly covered in the policy update [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines].

The policy update broadly states that providers must ask Medicaid members about other third-party coverage to ensure proper Coordination of Benefits, a detail not present in the provider manual chunk [New York State Medicaid Update April 2025 Volume 41 Number 4].

The provider manual provides a specific instruction for billing NYRx as the primary payer for non-work-related conditions, including an Eligibility Clarification Code, which is not present in the policy update [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines].

policy.title: New York State Medicaid Update April 2023 Volume 39 Number 8
policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

policy.text:
The pharmacy is responsible for using professional judgement in filling prescriptions without
regard to restriction requirements if the prescriptions were written as part of an emergency department visit
and the assigned PCP is unavailable (i.e., after hours) to verify the prescriptions. The pharmacy is responsible
for contacting the assigned PCP the next business day regarding the prescriptions obtained by the recipient.
An assigned primary inpatient hospital is responsible for providing all non-emergency inpatient services to the
restricted recipient except for services provided pursuant to an authorized referral.
Emergency Pharmacy Situations
In the event of an emergency, such as drug shortages, unexpected pharmacy closures, and/or unexpected
travel, providers and pharmacies must refer to the instructions and contact information provided below.
During normal business hours, the restricted recipient can obtain a temporary authorization to fill the
prescription at an alternate pharmacy location by contacting:
• the New York State (NYS) Office of the Medicaid Inspector General (OMIG)
Call (518) 474-6866 or email omig.sm.rrp@omig.ny.gov (available Monday through Friday
from 8 a.m.
policy.title: New York State Medicaid Update January 2025 Volume 41 Number 1
policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

policy.text:
Providers should verify the prescription directions for use and consult the prescriber, as necessary, on any
changes.
policy.title: New York State Medicaid Update September 2024 Volume 40 Number 10
policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

policy.text:
Reminder: Submitting for Prior Authorization of Non-Preferred Drugs
Prior authorization (PA) requests for non-preferred drugs must be submitted by the prescriber or by an
authorized agent of the prescriber. An authorized agent of the prescriber is considered an employee of the
prescribing practitioner and has access to the patient's medical records (i.e., nurse, medical assistant).
When presented with a prescription for a non-preferred drug that requires PA, the pharmacist must notify
the prescriber that PA is required and notify the patient once the prescriber has obtained the PA. If the
pharmacist is unable to reach the prescriber and a PA number has not yet been obtained by the prescriber,
the pharmacist may initiate a PA for a 72-hour emergency supply only.
Pharmacists are not authorized to submit for a PA nor enter into an agreement with a prescriber to
effectuate PAs and appeals on the prescriber’s behalf (except for the 72-hour emergency supply mentioned
above or when employed by a prescriber).
policy.title: New York State Medicaid Update July 2023 Volume 39 Number 12
policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
If the address, age, or CIN of the Medicaid member are missing, the pharmacist is not required to enter any of these
items on the prescription if the information:

• Is otherwise readily available in the records of the pharmacy and the pharmacist knows the person who is
requesting that the prescription be filled, or
• The pharmacist is otherwise satisfied that the prescription is legitimate.
Prescriptions written for controlled substances must meet the requirements of Article 33 of the Public Health Law. In
accordance with New York State Department of Health Codes, Rules, and Regulations Title 10, Part 80, pharmacists are
permitted to add or change only certain information on controlled substance prescriptions.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
• Providers must ensure that a pharmacist counsels the patient consistent with federal (including but not
limited to 42 CFR § 456.705(c) and SSA § 1927 (g)(2)(A)(ii)) and State law, rule, or regulation (8 NYCRR §
63.6(8)), and retain records for six years of provision of counseling in compliance with federal and State law,
rule, or regulation.
• Delivery confirmation must be maintained by the pharmacy for six years from the date of payment and
must be retrievable upon audit.
• All shipping and delivery costs are the responsibility of the pharmacy.
• Medicaid members cannot be charged for delivery if Medicaid reimburses for all, or any portion of the item
being delivered.
• The pharmacy is accountable for proper delivery of intact, usable product. Replacement for any recalled,
defective, or otherwise unusable product is the responsibility of the provider.
• The pharmacy is liable for the cost of any item damaged or lost through distribution and delivery.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
Pick Up / Receipt
• Pharmacies/DME providers must obtain a legible signature from the Medicaid member, their caregiver, or
their designee to confirm receipt of the prescription drugs, over-the-counter products, medical/surgical
supplies, and DME items when picked up from the provider. The pharmacy must have documentation
confirming the prescription number(s), date of pick-up and signature. One signature is sufficient for
multiple prescriptions being picked up at one time. Claim submission is not proof that the prescription or
fiscal order was actually furnished.
• Providers must ensure that a pharmacist counsel the patient consistent with federal (including but not
limited to 42 CFR § 456.705(c) and SSA § 1927 (g)(2)(A)(ii)) and State law, rule, or regulation (8 NYCRR §
63.6(8)), and retain records for six years of provision of counseling in compliance with federal and State law,
rule, or regulation. Reminder, counseling must include proper storage and use of the drug or item
dispensed.
• The pharmacy is accountable to provide intact, usable product. Replacement for any recalled, defective, or
otherwise unusable product is the responsibility of the provider.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
The pharmacist is responsible for making a good faith effort to verify the validity of the prescription and the
prescriber’s identity if the prescriber is unknown to the pharmacist. Electronic imaging of prescriptions and fiscal
orders must result in an exact reproduction of the original order and may be required to be authenticated.
Storage Requirements – Vaccine and Medication
Medicaid Pharmacy providers are required to follow Regulations set forth by the Commissioner of Education,
section 63.6(b)(3) and the CDC guidelines in the CDC Storage and Handling Toolkit for vaccines and medication
requiring cold storage. Pharmacies must have a purpose-built or pharmaceutical grade refrigerator with adequate
storage space with fan-forced air circulation; a dorm-style or bar-style unit with an evaporator plate/cooling coil
does not qualify.

Additionally, Medicaid providers who are part of the Vaccine for Children (VFC) program must meet criteria for
refrigeration and vaccine storage set forth in the guidelines outlined in the Stand-Alone Storage Unit Purchasing
Guidance and Temperature Monitoring Requirements documents.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

answers.headline_summary: Pharmacists have responsibilities to verify prescription legitimacy and prescriber identity, especially when information is missing. They are also responsible for notifying prescribers when prior authorization is required for non-preferred drugs and may initiate a 72-hour emergency supply PA if the prescriber is unreachable.

aligned_pairs.provider_manual: Pharmacists are not required to enter missing Medicaid member address, age, or CIN if the information is readily available in pharmacy records, the pharmacist knows the person, or is satisfied the prescription is legitimate. For controlled substances, pharmacists can only add or change certain information as per Title 10, Part 80. The pharmacist is also responsible for making a good faith effort to verify the validity of the prescription and the prescribers identity if the prescriber is unknown.
aligned_pairs.policy_update: Providers should verify prescription directions for use and consult the prescriber as necessary regarding any changes. This policy is broader as it does not specifically address missing patient or prescriber information but rather the general need to verify prescription details.

similarities: Both the provider manual and policy update emphasize the responsibility of pharmacists to verify prescription information and consult with the prescriber when necessary [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines, New York State Medicaid Update September 2024 Volume 40 Number 10].
differences: The provider manual specifically details pharmacist responsibilities when Medicaid member address, age, or CIN are missing, and outlines limitations for controlled substances, which is not covered in the policy update [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines].

The policy update broadly states the need to verify prescription directions for use, while the provider manual provides more specific scenarios regarding missing patient or prescriber identification information [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines, New York State Medicaid Update September 2024 Volume 40 Number 10].

answers.headline_summary: Pharmacists have responsibilities to verify prescription validity and prescriber identity, especially if the prescriber is unknown. When patient information like address, age, or CIN is missing, pharmacists can proceed if the information is readily available or they are satisfied the prescription is legitimate. For non-preferred drugs requiring prior authorization, pharmacists must notify the prescriber and may only initiate a 72-hour emergency supply PA themselves.

aligned_pairs.provider_manual: If the address, age, or CIN of a Medicaid member is missing, the pharmacist is not required to enter these items on the prescription. This applies if the information is readily available in the pharmacys records and the pharmacist knows the person. Alternatively, the pharmacist can proceed if they are otherwise satisfied that the prescription is legitimate.
aligned_pairs.policy_update: The pharmacy is responsible for using professional judgment when filling prescriptions without regard to restriction requirements. This applies if the prescriptions were written during an emergency department visit and the assigned Primary Care Provider (PCP) is unavailable. The pharmacy must then follow up with the assigned PCP the next business day regarding the prescriptions.

aligned_pairs.provider_manual: The pharmacist is responsible for making a good faith effort to verify the validity of the prescription. This includes verifying the prescribers identity if the prescriber is unknown to the pharmacist. Electronic imaging of prescriptions and fiscal orders must result in an exact reproduction of the original order.
aligned_pairs.policy_update: Providers should verify the prescription directions for use. They should also consult the prescriber, as necessary, on any changes. This ensures accuracy and appropriateness of the medication.

similarities: Both the provider manual and policy update emphasize the pharmacists responsibility to exercise professional judgment when information is missing or unclear [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines, New York State Medicaid Update January 2025 Volume 41 Number 1].
differences: The provider manual specifically addresses missing patient demographic information like address, age, or CIN, outlining conditions under which a pharmacist can still fill a prescription [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines].

The policy update focuses on situations where prescriptions are issued during an emergency department visit and the assigned PCP is unavailable, requiring follow-up the next business day [New York State Medicaid Update January 2025 Volume 41 Number 1].

The provider manual details the pharmacist's responsibility to verify the prescriber's identity if unknown, which is not explicitly mentioned in the policy update [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines].

aligned_pairs.provider_manual: If a Medicaid members address, age, or Client Identification Number (CIN) are missing, the pharmacist is not required to enter them if the information is readily available in pharmacy records. limited to 42 CFR § 456.705(c) and SSA § 1927 (g)(2)(A)(ii)) and State law, rule, or regulation (8 NYCRR §
63.6(8)), and retain records for six years of provision of counseling in compliance with federal and State law,
rule, or regulation.
• Delivery confirmation must be maintained by the pharmacy for six years from the date of payment and
must be retrievable upon audit.
• All shipping and delivery costs are the responsibility of the pharmacy.
• Medicaid members cannot be charged for delivery if Medicaid reimburses for all, or any portion of the item
being delivered.
• The pharmacy is accountable for proper delivery of intact, usable product. Replacement for any recalled,
defective, or otherwise unusable product is the responsibility of the provider.
• The pharmacy is liable for the cost of any item damaged or lost through distribution and delivery.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
Pick Up / Receipt
• Pharmacies/DME providers must obtain a legible signature from the Medicaid member, their caregiver, or
their designee to confirm receipt of the prescription drugs, over-the-counter products, medical/surgical
supplies, and DME items when picked up from the provider. The pharmacy must have documentation
confirming the prescription number(s), date of pick-up and signature. One signature is sufficient for
multiple prescriptions being picked up at one time. Claim submission is not proof that the prescription or
fiscal order was actually furnished.
• Providers must ensure that a pharmacist counsel the patient consistent with federal (including but not
limited to 42 CFR § 456.705(c) and SSA § 1927 (g)(2)(A)(ii)) and State law, rule, or regulation (8 NYCRR §
63.6(8)), and retain records for six years of provision of counseling in compliance with federal and State law,
rule, or regulation. Reminder, counseling must include proper storage and use of the drug or item
dispensed.
• The pharmacy is accountable to provide intact, usable product. Replacement for any recalled, defective, or
otherwise unusable product is the responsibility of the provider.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
The pharmacist is responsible for making a good faith effort to verify the validity of the prescription and the
prescriber’s identity if the prescriber is unknown to the pharmacist. Electronic imaging of prescriptions and fiscal
orders must result in an exact reproduction of the original order and may be required to be authenticated.
Storage Requirements – Vaccine and Medication
Medicaid Pharmacy providers are required to follow Regulations set forth by the Commissioner of Education,
section 63.6(b)(3) and the CDC guidelines in the CDC Storage and Handling Toolkit for vaccines and medication
requiring cold storage. Pharmacies must have a purpose-built or pharmaceutical grade refrigerator with adequate
storage space with fan-forced air circulation; a dorm-style or bar-style unit with an evaporator plate/cooling coil
does not qualify.

Additionally, Medicaid providers who are part of the Vaccine for Children (VFC) program must meet criteria for
refrigeration and vaccine storage set forth in the guidelines outlined in the Stand-Alone Storage Unit Purchasing
Guidance and Temperature Monitoring Requirements documents.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

answers.headline_summary: Pharmacists have responsibilities to verify prescription legitimacy and prescriber identity, especially when information is missing. They can fill prescriptions if certain patient details are readily available or if they are satisfied the prescription is legitimate, but must adhere to specific rules for controlled substances.

aligned_pairs.provider_manual: If a Medicaid member's address, age, or Client Identification Number (CIN) are missing, the pharmacist is not required to enter them if the information is readily available in the pharmacy's records. This also applies if the pharmacist knows the person requesting the prescription or is otherwise satisfied that the prescription is legitimate. However, prescriptions for controlled substances must comply with Article 33 of the Public Health Law, and pharmacists are only permitted to add or change specific information on these prescriptions.
aligned_pairs.policy_update: Pharmacists are responsible for exercising professional judgment when filling emergency department prescriptions, even if they do not meet restriction requirements, particularly if the assigned Primary Care Provider (PCP) is unavailable. The pharmacy must contact the assigned PCP on the next business day to discuss the prescriptions obtained by the recipient. This policy addresses situations where immediate dispensing is necessary due to an emergency department visit.

aligned_pairs.provider_manual: The pharmacist is responsible for making a good faith effort to verify the validity of a prescription. This includes confirming the prescriber’s identity, especially if the prescriber is unknown to the pharmacist. Electronic imaging of prescriptions and fiscal orders must produce an exact reproduction of the original order.
aligned_pairs.policy_update: Providers should verify the directions for use on a prescription. They must consult the prescriber as necessary regarding any changes to the prescription. This ensures accuracy and patient safety.

similarities: Both the provider manual and policy emphasize the pharmacists responsibility to exercise professional judgment when information is missing or unusual.

Both documents highlight the importance of verifying prescription details and, if necessary, consulting with the prescriber to ensure accuracy and validity [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines, New York State Medicaid Update September 2024 Volume 40 Number 10].
differences: The provider manual specifies conditions under which pharmacists are not required to enter missing patient demographic information, such as when it's readily available or the pharmacist is satisfied with the prescription's legitimacy, while the policy focuses on emergency department prescriptions and PCP unavailability [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines, New York State Medicaid Update January 2025 Volume 41 Number 1].

The provider manual provides specific guidance for controlled substances, noting that only certain information can be added or changed, which is not detailed in the policy update regarding missing information [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines].

test_id_40.query_content: What responsibilities do pharmacists have when required patient or prescriber information is missing?
test_id_40.concept: pharmacist responsibilities when required patient or prescriber information is missing

policy.title: New York State Medicaid Update April 2023 Volume 39 Number 8
policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

policy.text:
The pharmacy is responsible for using professional judgement in filling prescriptions without
regard to restriction requirements if the prescriptions were written as part of an emergency department visit
and the assigned PCP is unavailable (i.e., after hours) to verify the prescriptions. The pharmacy is responsible
for contacting the assigned PCP the next business day regarding the prescriptions obtained by the recipient.
An assigned primary inpatient hospital is responsible for providing all non-emergency inpatient services to the
restricted recipient except for services provided pursuant to an authorized referral.
Emergency Pharmacy Situations
In the event of an emergency, such as drug shortages, unexpected pharmacy closures, and/or unexpected
travel, providers and pharmacies must refer to the instructions and contact information provided below.
During normal business hours, the restricted recipient can obtain a temporary authorization to fill the
prescription at an alternate pharmacy location by contacting:
• the New York State (NYS) Office of the Medicaid Inspector General (OMIG)
Call (518) 474-6866 or email omig.sm.rrp@omig.ny.gov (available Monday through Friday
from 8 a.m.
policy.title: New York State Medicaid Update January 2025 Volume 41 Number 1
policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

policy.text:
Providers should verify the prescription directions for use and consult the prescriber, as necessary, on any
changes.
policy.title: New York State Medicaid Update September 2024 Volume 40 Number 10
policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

policy.text:
Reminder: Submitting for Prior Authorization of Non-Preferred Drugs
Prior authorization (PA) requests for non-preferred drugs must be submitted by the prescriber or by an
authorized agent of the prescriber. An authorized agent of the prescriber is considered an employee of the
prescribing practitioner and has access to the patient's medical records (i.e., nurse, medical assistant).
When presented with a prescription for a non-preferred drug that requires PA, the pharmacist must notify
the prescriber that PA is required and notify the patient once the prescriber has obtained the PA. If the
pharmacist is unable to reach the prescriber and a PA number has not yet been obtained by the prescriber,
the pharmacist may initiate a PA for a 72-hour emergency supply only.
Pharmacists are not authorized to submit for a PA nor enter into an agreement with a prescriber to
effectuate PAs and appeals on the prescriber’s behalf (except for the 72-hour emergency supply mentioned
above or when employed by a prescriber).
policy.title: New York State Medicaid Update July 2023 Volume 39 Number 12
policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
If the address, age, or CIN of the Medicaid member are missing, the pharmacist is not required to enter any of these
items on the prescription if the information:

• Is otherwise readily available in the records of the pharmacy and the pharmacist knows the person who is
requesting that the prescription be filled, or
• The pharmacist is otherwise satisfied that the prescription is legitimate.
Prescriptions written for controlled substances must meet the requirements of Article 33 of the Public Health Law. In
accordance with New York State Department of Health Codes, Rules, and Regulations Title 10, Part 80, pharmacists are
permitted to add or change only certain information on controlled substance prescriptions.

answers.headline_summary: Pharmacists have responsibilities to verify prescription validity and prescriber identity, and to use professional judgment when patient or prescriber information is missing, especially in emergency situations. They are also responsible for notifying prescribers when prior authorization is required for non-preferred drugs.

aligned_pairs.provider_manual: If a Medicaid members address, age, or Client Identification Number (CIN) are missing, the pharmacist is not required to enter these items. This applies if the information is readily available in pharmacy records, the pharmacist knows the person, or is satisfied the prescription is legitimate. However, prescriptions for controlled substances must adhere to specific Public Health Law requirements, and pharmacists can only add or change certain information.
aligned_pairs.policy_update: The pharmacy is responsible for exercising professional judgment when filling prescriptions, even if restriction requirements are not met. This applies if the prescriptions were issued during an emergency department visit and the assigned Primary Care Provider (PCP) is unavailable. The pharmacy must then contact the assigned PCP the next business day regarding these prescriptions.

aligned_pairs.provider_manual: The pharmacist is responsible for making a good faith effort to verify the validity of the prescription. This includes confirming the prescribers identity if the prescriber is unknown to the pharmacist. Electronic images of prescriptions and fiscal orders must be exact reproductions of the original and may require authentication.
aligned_pairs.policy_update: Providers should verify the prescription directions for use. They must also consult the prescriber as necessary regarding any changes. This ensures clarity and accuracy in medication dispensing.

similarities: Both the provider manual and policy update emphasize the pharmacists responsibility to exercise professional judgment when information is missing or unclear [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines, New York State Medicaid Update January 2025 Volume 41 Number 1].

Both documents highlight the importance of verifying prescription details and, if necessary, consulting with the prescriber [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines, New York State Medicaid Update September 2024 Volume 40 Number 10].
differences: The provider manual specifically addresses missing patient demographic information like address, age, or CIN, and outlines conditions under which a pharmacist can proceed without entering this data [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines].

The policy update focuses on emergency situations, such as prescriptions from an emergency department visit when the PCP is unavailable, and requires follow-up with the PCP the next business day [New York State Medicaid Update January 2025 Volume 41 Number 1].

The provider manual details specific limitations on adding or changing information for controlled substance prescriptions, which is not mentioned in the policy update [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines].

test_id_41.query_content: Are weight loss drugs an excluded benefit?
test_id_41.concept: weight loss drugs excluded benefit status

policy.text:
This article serves as a reminder of those exclusions for both the FFS and MMC program areas, which are
as follows:
• agents when used for the treatment of anorexia, weight loss or weight gain [additionally pursuant to
New York Code, Rules and Regulations (NYCRR) Title 18 §505.3(g)(3)];
• agents when used for the treatment of sexual dysfunction unless such agents are used to treat a
condition, other than sexual or erectile dysfunction, for which the agents have been approved by the
Food and Drug Administration (FDA) [additionally pursuant to New York Social Services Law §365-
a(4)(f)]; and
• agents when used for cosmetic use or hair growth (additionally pursuant to NYCRR Title 18
§505.2(l)(5)).
policy.title: New York State Medicaid Update April 2022 Volume 38 Number 4
policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

policy.text:
April 2024 New York State Medicaid Update pg. 6

• Compounds may not contain excluded drugs or be made for NYS Medicaid excluded indications as
per the Social Security Act §1927(d)(2), located at: https://www.ssa.gov/OP_Home/ssact/title
19/1927.htm, including, but not limited to drugs to treat weight loss or sexual dysfunction or for
cosmetic purposes.
• Compounds may not be made to bypass the criteria within the NYRx, the Medicaid Pharmacy Program
Preferred Drug List, located at: https://newyork.fhsc.com/downloads/providers/NYRx_PDP_PDL.pdf.
• Compounds may not be made with or to replace drug products removed from the marketplace due to
safety reasons.
• Compounds may not be made in therapeutic amounts or combinations not FDA-approved or
compendia-supported for use.
• Compounds may not be made to add coloring, flavoring, perfumes, or other non-active ingredient
additives to a commercially available product.
• Submitted compound claims may not include packaging materials or containers, syringes, or other
items utilized or necessary in the preparation or use of final compounded product.
policy.title: New York State Medicaid Update April 2024 Volume 40 Number 4
policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

policy.text:
December 2020 New York State Medicaid Update pg. 9

• Compounds may not contain drugs or be made for NYS Medicaid excluded indications as per the
Social Security Act §1927(d)(2) including but not limited to drugs to treat weight loss or sexual
dysfunction or for cosmetic purposes.
• Compounds may not be made in therapeutic amounts or combinations not FDA-approved, or
compendia-supported for use.
• Compounds may not be made to add coloring, flavoring, perfumes or other non-active ingredient
additives to a commercially available product.
• Submitted compound claims may not include packaging materials or containers, syringes, or other
items utilized or necessary in the preparation of final compounded product.
• Compounds must be adjudicated with the appropriate and matching final compounded product route
code for the dosage form.
• Prepared compounds that mimic a commercial product must include on the prescription and in the
members medical chart documentation of the reason for compounding (i.e., sensitivity or
contraindication to dyes, preservatives, or fillers or lack of availability of a commercial product).
policy.title: New York State Medicaid Update December 2020 Volume 36 Number 18
policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

policy.text:
*Requires additional MEVS.
Denial code “722”: NDC DESI code is invalid.
NDC Excluded from State Plan Coverage
NYS Medicaid only provides reimbursement for drugs included on the New York State Department of Health
List of Medicaid Reimbursable Drugs, located at: https://www.emedny.org/info/formfile.aspx (unless provided
by a facility which includes the cost of drugs in their all-inclusive rate). The following are examples of
drugs/drug uses which are not reimbursable by NYS Medicaid in accordance with policy and/or state or federal
legislation:
• Drugs used for the treatment of anorexia, weight loss or weight gain pursuant to SSA §1927(d)(2),
located at: https://www.ssa.gov/OP_Home/ssact/title19/1927.htm.
• Drugs for the treatment of sexual dysfunction pursuant to SSA §1927(d)(2), located at: https://
www.ssa.gov/OP_Home/ssact/title19/1927.htm, and SOS §365-a(4)(f), located at: https://www.
nysenate.gov/legislation/laws/SOS/365-A.
• Drugs indicated for cosmetic use or hair growth pursuant to SSA §1927(d)(2), located at: https://
www.ssa.gov/OP_Home/ssact/title19/1927.htm.
policy.title: New York State Medicaid Update January 2025 Volume 41 Number 1
policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
• Refills of compounds must be specifically requested by the patient or patient´s designee before the item is
prepared and submitted for payment.
• Ingredients in a compound claim will only be reimbursed if available on the NYS Medicaid List of Reimbursable
Drugs.
• Ingredients will only be reimbursed if they are compendia-supported for the intended route of administration
and indication.
• Compounds may not contain excluded drugs or be made for NYS Medicaid excluded indications as per the
Social Security Act §1927(d)(2), including, but not limited to drugs to treat weight loss or sexual dysfunction or
for cosmetic purposes.
• Compounds may not be made to bypass the criteria within the NYS Medicaid Fee-for-Service Programs.
• Compounds may not be made with or to replace drug products removed from the marketplace due to safety
reasons.
• Compounds may not be made in therapeutic amounts or combinations not FDA-approved, or compendia-
supported for use.
• Compounds may not be made to add coloring, flavoring, perfumes, or other non-active ingredient additives to
a commercially available product.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
Note: Reimbursable drugs are listed alphabetically in sections by Rx Type.
Drug Coverage Limitations
Medicaid only provides reimbursement for drugs included on the New York State List of Medicaid Reimbursable Drugs
(unless provided by a facility which includes the cost of drugs in their all-inclusive rate). The following are examples of
drugs/drug uses which are not reimbursable by Medicaid in accordance with Policy and/or state or federal Legislation:
• Drugs used for the treatment of anorexia, weight loss or weight gain pursuant to SSA §1927(d)(2).
• Drugs for the treatment of sexual dysfunction pursuant to SSA §1927(d)(2), and Social Services Law §365-a(4)(f).
• Drugs without a federal rebate agreement pursuant to SSA §1927(a).
• Drugs indicated for cosmetic use or hair growth pursuant to SSA §1927(d)(2).
• Drugs that are not approved as safe and effective by the FDA pursuant to SSA §1927(d)(4)(C).
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
It is not appropriate to bill
Medicaid for any prescription drug for a Medicare Part D member unless as stated below.
Under the Medicare Part D prescription drug benefit most drug costs are paid for by Medicare. Medicaid only pays for
drugs from the List of Medicaid Reimbursable Drugs where the drug class is specifically excluded by law from being
covered under the Part D plans, such as:
• Select prescription vitamins. and
• Certain non-prescription drugs.
All claims for dual-eligible members submitted to Medicaid are subject to Medicaid rules, including prior authorization.

For more information regarding Medicare Part D benefit, refer to the DOH website at:
https://www.health.ny.gov/health_care/medicaid/program/medicaid_transition/index.htm.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
340B Pharmacy Drug Claims
Upon enrollment in the 340B program, 340B covered entities must determine whether they will use 340B drugs for
their Medicaid patients.
Federal law prohibits duplicate discounts – manufacturers are not required to provide a discounted 340B price and a
Medicaid drug rebate for the same drug. The federal Health Resources and Services Administration (HRSA) has rules
and requirements for when a covered entity uses 340B drugs for Medicaid members, including listing the entity’s
Medicaid provider number/NPI on the HRSA Medicaid Exclusion File (MEF). Information on HRSA’s requirements in this
area can be found at the following site: http://www.hrsa.gov/opa/programrequirements/medicaidexclusion/index.html.
The NY Medicaid program does not use HRSA’s Medicaid Exclusion File. NY Medicaid relies completely on the use of
340B claim level identifiers to avoid duplicate discounts. These identifiers are required at the claim submission level for
all 340B drug claims, thereby avoiding duplicate discounts. Using these identifiers is the only way NY Medicaid will
remove the claim from rebate invoicing.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

answers.headline_summary: New York State Medicaid generally excludes drugs used for weight loss from reimbursement, as per federal and state legislation. This exclusion applies to both direct drug coverage and ingredients in compounded medications.

aligned_pairs.provider_manual: Compounded medications may not contain excluded drugs or be made for New York State Medicaid excluded indications. This includes drugs to treat weight loss, as specified by the Social Security Act §1927(d)(2).
aligned_pairs.policy_update: Compounded medications may not contain excluded drugs or be made for New York State Medicaid excluded indications. This includes drugs to treat weight loss or sexual dysfunction, as outlined in the Social Security Act §1927(d)(2).

aligned_pairs.provider_manual: Medicaid does not reimburse for drugs used for the treatment of anorexia, weight loss, or weight gain. This exclusion is pursuant to the Social Security Act §1927(d)(2).
aligned_pairs.policy_update: New York State Medicaid does not reimburse for drugs used for the treatment of anorexia, weight loss, or weight gain. This policy is in accordance with the Social Security Act §1927(d)(2).

similarities: Both the provider manual and policy update explicitly state that compounded medications cannot contain drugs for weight loss due to the Social Security Act §1927(d)(2) [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines, New York State Medicaid Update April 2024 Volume 40 Number 4].

Both sources confirm that drugs for the treatment of anorexia, weight loss, or weight gain are not reimbursable by Medicaid, citing the same federal statute, Social Security Act §1927(d)(2) [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines, New York State Medicaid Update January 2025 Volume 41 Number 1].

a commercially available product.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
Note: Reimbursable drugs are listed alphabetically in sections by Rx Type.
Drug Coverage Limitations
Medicaid only provides reimbursement for drugs included on the New York State List of Medicaid Reimbursable Drugs
(unless provided by a facility which includes the cost of drugs in their all-inclusive rate). The following are examples of
drugs/drug uses which are not reimbursable by Medicaid in accordance with Policy and/or state or federal Legislation:
• Drugs used for the treatment of anorexia, weight loss or weight gain pursuant to SSA §1927(d)(2).
• Drugs for the treatment of sexual dysfunction pursuant to SSA §1927(d)(2), and Social Services Law §365-a(4)(f).
• Drugs without a federal rebate agreement pursuant to SSA §1927(a).
• Drugs indicated for cosmetic use or hair growth pursuant to SSA §1927(d)(2).
• Drugs that are not approved as safe and effective by the FDA pursuant to SSA §1927(d)(4)(C).
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
It is not appropriate to bill
Medicaid for any prescription drug for a Medicare Part D member unless as stated below.
Under the Medicare Part D prescription drug benefit most drug costs are paid for by Medicare. Medicaid only pays for
drugs from the List of Medicaid Reimbursable Drugs where the drug class is specifically excluded by law from being
covered under the Part D plans, such as:
• Select prescription vitamins. and
• Certain non-prescription drugs.
All claims for dual-eligible members submitted to Medicaid are subject to Medicaid rules, including prior authorization.

For more information regarding Medicare Part D benefit, refer to the DOH website at:
https://www.health.ny.gov/health_care/medicaid/program/medicaid_transition/index.htm.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
340B Pharmacy Drug Claims
Upon enrollment in the 340B program, 340B covered entities must determine whether they will use 340B drugs for
their Medicaid patients.
Federal law prohibits duplicate discounts – manufacturers are not required to provide a discounted 340B price and a
Medicaid drug rebate for the same drug. The federal Health Resources and Services Administration (HRSA) has rules
and requirements for when a covered entity uses 340B drugs for Medicaid members, including listing the entity’s
Medicaid provider number/NPI on the HRSA Medicaid Exclusion File (MEF). Information on HRSA’s requirements in this
area can be found at the following site: http://www.hrsa.gov/opa/programrequirements/medicaidexclusion/index.html.
The NY Medicaid program does not use HRSA’s Medicaid Exclusion File. NY Medicaid relies completely on the use of
340B claim level identifiers to avoid duplicate discounts. These identifiers are required at the claim submission level for
all 340B drug claims, thereby avoiding duplicate discounts. Using these identifiers is the only way NY Medicaid will
remove the claim from rebate invoicing.

provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

policy.text:
This article serves as a reminder of those exclusions for both the FFS and MMC program areas, which are
as follows:
• agents when used for the treatment of anorexia, weight loss or weight gain [additionally pursuant to
New York Code, Rules and Regulations (NYCRR) Title 18 §505.3(g)(3)];
• agents when used for the treatment of sexual dysfunction unless such agents are used to treat a
condition, other than sexual or erectile dysfunction, for which the agents have been approved by the
Food and Drug Administration (FDA) [additionally pursuant to New York Social Services Law §365-
a(4)(f)]; and
• agents when used for cosmetic use or hair growth (additionally pursuant to NYCRR Title 18
§505.2(l)(5)).
policy.title: New York State Medicaid Update April 2022 Volume 38 Number 4
policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

policy.text:
April 2024 New York State Medicaid Update pg. 6

• Compounds may not contain excluded drugs or be made for NYS Medicaid excluded indications as
per the Social Security Act §1927(d)(2), located at: https://www.ssa.gov/OP_Home/ssact/title
19/1927.htm, including, but not limited to drugs to treat weight loss or sexual dysfunction or for
cosmetic purposes.
• Compounds may not be made to bypass the criteria within the NYRx, the Medicaid Pharmacy Program
Preferred Drug List, located at: https://newyork.fhsc.com/downloads/providers/NYRx_PDP_PDL.pdf.
• Compounds may not be made with or to replace drug products removed from the marketplace due to
safety reasons.
• Compounds may not be made in therapeutic amounts or combinations not FDA-approved or
compendia-supported for use.
• Compounds may not be made to add coloring, flavoring, perfumes, or other non-active ingredient
additives to a commercially available product.
• Submitted compound claims may not include packaging materials or containers, syringes, or other
items utilized or necessary in the preparation or use of final compounded product.
policy.title: New York State Medicaid Update April 2024 Volume 40 Number 4
policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

policy.text:
December 2020 New York State Medicaid Update pg. 9

• Compounds may not contain drugs or be made for NYS Medicaid excluded indications as per the
Social Security Act §1927(d)(2) including but not limited to drugs to treat weight loss or sexual
dysfunction or for cosmetic purposes.
• Compounds may not be made in therapeutic amounts or combinations not FDA-approved, or
compendia-supported for use.
• Compounds may not be made to add coloring, flavoring, perfumes or other non-active ingredient
additives to a commercially available product.
• Submitted compound claims may not include packaging materials or containers, syringes, or other
items utilized or necessary in the preparation of final compounded product.
• Compounds must be adjudicated with the appropriate and matching final compounded product route
code for the dosage form.
• Prepared compounds that mimic a commercial product must include on the prescription and in the
members medical chart documentation of the reason for compounding (i.e., sensitivity or
contraindication to dyes, preservatives, or fillers or lack of availability of a commercial product).

provider_manual.text:
• Refills of compounds must be specifically requested by the patient or patient´s designee before the item is
prepared and submitted for payment.

Drugs.

and indication.

for cosmetic purposes.

reasons.

supported for use.
• Compounds may not be made to add coloring, flavoring, perfumes, or other non-active ingredient additives to
a commercially available product.

• Compounds may not contain excluded drugs or be made for NYS Medicaid excluded indications as per the
Social Security Act §1927(d)(2), including, but not limited to drugs to treat weight loss or sexual dysfunction or
for cosmetic purposes.
• Compounds may not be made to bypass the criteria within the NYS Medicaid Fee-for-Service Programs.
• Compounds may not be made with or to replace drug products removed from the marketplace due to safety
reasons.
• Compounds may not be made in therapeutic amounts or combinations not FDA-approved, or compendia-
supported for use.
• Compounds may not be made to add coloring, flavoring, perfumes, or other non-active ingredient additives to
a commercially available product.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
Note: Reimbursable drugs are listed alphabetically in sections by Rx Type.
Drug Coverage Limitations
Medicaid only provides reimbursement for drugs included on the New York State List of Medicaid Reimbursable Drugs
(unless provided by a facility which includes the cost of drugs in their all-inclusive rate). The following are examples of
drugs/drug uses which are not reimbursable by Medicaid in accordance with Policy and/or state or federal Legislation:
• Drugs used for the treatment of anorexia, weight loss or weight gain pursuant to SSA §1927(d)(2).
• Drugs for the treatment of sexual dysfunction pursuant to SSA §1927(d)(2), and Social Services Law §365-a(4)(f).
• Drugs without a federal rebate agreement pursuant to SSA §1927(a).
• Drugs indicated for cosmetic use or hair growth pursuant to SSA §1927(d)(2).
• Drugs that are not approved as safe and effective by the FDA pursuant to SSA §1927(d)(4)(C).
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
It is not appropriate to bill
Medicaid for any prescription drug for a Medicare Part D member unless as stated below.
Under the Medicare Part D prescription drug benefit most drug costs are paid for by Medicare. Medicaid only pays for
drugs from the List of Medicaid Reimbursable Drugs where the drug class is specifically excluded by law from being
covered under the Part D plans, such as:
• Select prescription vitamins. and
• Certain non-prescription drugs.
All claims for dual-eligible members submitted to Medicaid are subject to Medicaid rules, including prior authorization.

For more information regarding Medicare Part D benefit, refer to the DOH website at:
https://www.health.ny.gov/health_care/medicaid/program/medicaid_transition/index.htm.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
340B Pharmacy Drug Claims
Upon enrollment in the 340B program, 340B covered entities must determine whether they will use 340B drugs for
their Medicaid patients.
Federal law prohibits duplicate discounts – manufacturers are not required to provide a discounted 340B price and a
Medicaid drug rebate for the same drug. The federal Health Resources and Services Administration (HRSA) has rules
and requirements for when a covered entity uses 340B drugs for Medicaid members, including listing the entity’s
Medicaid provider number/NPI on the HRSA Medicaid Exclusion File (MEF). Information on HRSA’s requirements in this
area can be found at the following site: http://www.hrsa.gov/opa/programrequirements/medicaidexclusion/index.html.
The NY Medicaid program does not use HRSA’s Medicaid Exclusion File. NY Medicaid relies completely on the use of
340B claim level identifiers to avoid duplicate discounts. These identifiers are required at the claim submission level for
all 340B drug claims, thereby avoiding duplicate discounts. Using these identifiers is the only way NY Medicaid will
remove the claim from rebate invoicing.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

answers.headline_summary: New York State Medicaid generally excludes drugs used for weight loss from reimbursement, as per federal and state legislation. This exclusion applies to both direct drug coverage and ingredients in compounded medications.

aligned_pairs.provider_manual: Compounded medications may not contain excluded drugs or be made for New York State Medicaid excluded indications. This includes drugs to treat weight loss, as specified by the Social Security Act §1927(d)(2).
aligned_pairs.policy_update: Compounded medications may not contain excluded drugs or be made for New York State Medicaid excluded indications. This includes drugs to treat weight loss or sexual dysfunction, as per the Social Security Act §1927(d)(2).

aligned_pairs.provider_manual: Medicaid does not reimburse for drugs used for the treatment of anorexia, weight loss, or weight gain. This exclusion is pursuant to the Social Security Act §1927(d)(2).
aligned_pairs.policy_update: New York State Medicaid does not reimburse for drugs used for the treatment of anorexia, weight loss, or weight gain. This policy is in accordance with the Social Security Act §1927(d)(2).

similarities: Both the provider manual and policy update state that compounded medications cannot contain drugs for weight loss due to the Social Security Act §1927(d)(2) [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines, New York State Medicaid Update April 2024 Volume 40 Number 4].

Both sources explicitly exclude drugs for the treatment of anorexia, weight loss, or weight gain from Medicaid reimbursement, citing the same federal statute, Social Security Act §1927(d)(2) [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines, New York State Medicaid Update January 2025 Volume 41 Number 1].

policy.text:
National Drug Code Reporting Requirements
Providers are required to report the National Drug Code (NDC) for all PADs billed by a private practitioner to
NYS Medicaid and for all ordered ambulatory claims billed by clinics. If the NDC is not referenced on a claim,
NYS Medicaid will not provide reimbursement for the drug.
Drugs obtained via the 340B program must be identified by appending the UD modifier to the 340B drug line
on submitted NYS Medicaid FFS-only claims. Providers are required to report the NDC on FFS claims for the
reimbursement of 340B drugs. Providers must also include the number of units administered and their actual
acquisition cost, by invoice, in the charges field of a claim. Please note: Medicare requires either the JG or
TB modifiers be appended to the 340B drug line for Medicare/NYS Medicaid crossover claims (no UD modifier
is needed on crossover claims).
An accurate NDC must be reported for all PADs billed to NYS Medicaid FFS on an institutional claim that
uses Ambulatory Patient Groups (APGs) payment methodology.

policy.text:
New System Edit to be Implemented to Validate the
Ingredient Cost for 340B Drugs
As previously communicated in the January 2018 and December 2018 Medicaid Update publications, 340B drug
claims submitted to Medicaid via the National Council for Prescription Drug Programs (NCPDP) D.0 format are:
• required to be properly identified as 340B for both fee-for-service (FFS) and Medicaid managed care
(MMC) members; as well as
• submitted at the 340B acquisition cost by invoice to the provider for FFS members, net any manufacturer
discounts and/or other price reductions.
The following fields are required on Medicaid 340B drug claims submitted via NCPDP:
Medicaid Primary Medicaid Secondary Claim
Field
Claim (Primary: Medicare; Commercial)
420-DK, Submission Clarification Code (SCC) 20 20
423-DN, Basis of Cost Determination (BCD)* 08 No Requirements Specific to Medicaid
409-D9, Ingredient Cost Submitted* 340B Acquisition No Requirements Specific to Medicaid
Cost
426-DQ, Usual and Customary Cost (U&C) ** Lowest Net Charge Lowest Net Charge to Cash Customers
to Cash Customers
*MMC plans should be consulted on their requirements for this field.
policy.title: New York State Medicaid Update August 2019 Volume 35 Number 9
policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

policy.text:
Any pharmacy that submits a 340B drug claim, whenever the ingredient cost submitted is higher than the ceiling
price, will be returned the Medicaid Eligibility Verification System (MEVS) Rx Denial code “708: Exceeds NY
Allowed Maximum” and the NCPDP Reject code "23: M/I Ingredient Cost.” The pharmacy would then have
to resubmit the claim with the correct ingredient cost.

provider_manual.text:
340B Pharmacy Drug Claims
Upon enrollment in the 340B program, 340B covered entities must determine whether they will use 340B drugs for
their Medicaid patients.
Federal law prohibits duplicate discounts – manufacturers are not required to provide a discounted 340B price and a
Medicaid drug rebate for the same drug. The federal Health Resources and Services Administration (HRSA) has rules
and requirements for when a covered entity uses 340B drugs for Medicaid members, including listing the entity’s

The NY Medicaid program does not use HRSA’s Medicaid Exclusion File. NY Medicaid relies completely on the use of

provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
6.0 Definitions
The following terms are defined for the purposes of the NY Medicaid program and are included to help clarify policies
as provided in this Manual:
340B Ceiling Price
The 340B ceiling price is statutorily defined as the Average Manufacturer Price (AMP) reduced by the rebate
percentage, which is commonly referred to as the Unit Rebate Amount (URA). HRSA obtains the AMP and URA data
from the Centers for Medicare and Medicaid Services (CMS) as part of quarterly reporting for the Medicaid Drug
Rebate Program. This figure is then multiplied by the package size and case package size to produce a price that is
used in the marketplace for purchasing covered outpatient drugs. For example, the AMP minus the URA indicates the
cost of one pill.
Actual Acquisition Cost
Refers to the price paid for a drug or supply by the provider or in the case of a Covered Agency for a 340B purchased
drug, from a supplier with no added costs, delivery fees or dispensing fees, or means the agency's determination of the
pharmacy providers' actual prices paid to acquire drug products marketed or sold by specific manufacturers.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
Note: applying claim level identifiers on non-340B purchased drug claims is
considered fraudulent billing.
The following fields are required on Medicaid 340B drug claims submitted in the NCPDP format:
Medicaid Secondary Claim
Field Medicaid Primary Claim (Medicare. Commercial)
420-DK, Submission Clarification Code (SCC) 20 20
423-DN, Basis of Cost Determination (BCD) 08 N/A
409-D9 Ingredient Cost Submitted 340B Acquisition Cost N/A
Lowest Net Charge to Cash Lowest Net Charge to Cash
426-DQ Usual and Customary Cost (U&C) Customers Customers
FAQs on the 340B program itself, as well as information on how to ask additional questions, can be found on the HRSA
website at: https://www.hrsa.gov/opa/faqs/index.html.
