provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

answers.headline_summary: 340B drugs are reimbursed based on their acquisition cost. Claims for these drugs must include specific identifiers and the 340B acquisition cost to avoid duplicate discounts and ensure proper reimbursement.

aligned_pairs.provider_manual: For 340B purchased drugs, reimbursement is based on the 340B Acquisition Cost. A professional dispensing fee of $10.18 is also applied, unless the pharmacys Usual and Customary (U&C) pricing is lower.
aligned_pairs.policy_update: For 340B drugs, providers must report the National Drug Code (NDC) and append the UD modifier to the drug line on fee-for-service (FFS)-only claims. The actual acquisition cost, supported by an invoice, must be included in the charges field for reimbursement.

aligned_pairs.provider_manual: Federal law prohibits duplicate discounts for 340B drugs, meaning manufacturers are not required to provide both a discounted 340B price and a Medicaid drug rebate for the same drug. The New York Medicaid program relies on claim-level identifiers to prevent these duplicate discounts. These identifiers are required for all 340B drug claims to remove them from rebate invoicing.
aligned_pairs.policy_update: Drugs obtained through the 340B program must be identified by appending the UD modifier to the 340B drug line on submitted New York State Medicaid fee-for-service (FFS)-only claims. This identification ensures proper reimbursement and helps avoid issues such as duplicate discounts.

aligned_pairs.provider_manual: Medicaid 340B drug claims submitted in NCPDP format require specific fields to be completed. These include Submission Clarification Code (SCC) 20, Basis of Cost Determination (BCD) 08, and Ingredient Cost Submitted as 340B Acquisition Cost. Additionally, the Usual and Customary Cost (U&C) must be reported as the Lowest Net Charge to Cash Customers.
aligned_pairs.policy_update: 340B drug claims submitted via NCPDP D.0 format must be identified as 340B and submitted at the 340B acquisition cost by invoice. Required fields include SCC 20, BCD 08, Ingredient Cost Submitted as 340B Acquisition Cost, and U&C as Lowest Net Charge to Cash Customers.

similarities: Both the provider manual and policy update state that reimbursement for 340B drugs is based on the 340B acquisition cost [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines, New York State Medicaid Update July 2022 Volume 38 Number 8].

Both sources emphasize the importance of preventing duplicate discounts for 340B drugs [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines, New York State Medicaid Update July 2022 Volume 38 Number 8].

Both the provider manual and policy update specify the same required fields for 340B drug claims submitted in NCPDP format, including SCC 20, BCD 08, Ingredient Cost Submitted as 340B Acquisition Cost, and U&C as Lowest Net Charge to Cash Customers [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines, New York State Medicaid Update August 2019 Volume 35 Number 9].
differences: The provider manual explicitly states a professional dispensing fee of $10.18 for 340B purchased drugs, which is not detailed in the policy update [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines].

The policy update specifically mentions the requirement to report the National Drug Code (NDC) and append the UD modifier for 340B drug lines on FFS-only claims, which is not explicitly stated in the provider manuals general reimbursement methodology section [New York State Medicaid Update July 2022 Volume 38 Number 8].

policy.text:
New System Edit to be Implemented to Validate the
Ingredient Cost for 340B Drugs
As previously communicated in the January 2018 and December 2018 Medicaid Update publications, 340B drug
claims submitted to Medicaid via the National Council for Prescription Drug Programs (NCPDP) D.0 format are:
• required to be properly identified as 340B for both fee-for-service (FFS) and Medicaid managed care
(MMC) members; as well as
• submitted at the 340B acquisition cost by invoice to the provider for FFS members, net any manufacturer
discounts and/or other price reductions.
The following fields are required on Medicaid 340B drug claims submitted via NCPDP:
Medicaid Primary Medicaid Secondary Claim
Field
Claim (Primary: Medicare; Commercial)
420-DK, Submission Clarification Code (SCC) 20 20
423-DN, Basis of Cost Determination (BCD)* 08 No Requirements Specific to Medicaid
409-D9, Ingredient Cost Submitted* 340B Acquisition No Requirements Specific to Medicaid
Cost
426-DQ, Usual and Customary Cost (U&C) ** Lowest Net Charge Lowest Net Charge to Cash Customers
to Cash Customers
*MMC plans should be consulted on their requirements for this field.

policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

price, will be returned the Medicaid Eligibility Verification System (MEVS) Rx Denial code “708: Exceeds NY
Allowed Maximum” and the NCPDP Reject code "23: M/I Ingredient Cost.” The pharmacy would then have
to resubmit the claim with the correct ingredient cost.

provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
340B Pharmacy Drug Claims
Upon enrollment in the 340B program, 340B covered entities must determine whether they will use 340B drugs for
their Medicaid patients.
Federal law prohibits duplicate discounts – manufacturers are not required to provide a discounted 340B price and a
Medicaid drug rebate for the same drug. The federal Health Resources and Services Administration (HRSA) has rules
and requirements for when a covered entity uses 340B drugs for Medicaid members, including listing the entity’s
Medicaid provider number/NPI on the HRSA Medicaid Exclusion File (MEF). Information on HRSA’s requirements in this
area can be found at the following site: http://www.hrsa.gov/opa/programrequirements/medicaidexclusion/index.html.
The NY Medicaid program does not use HRSA’s Medicaid Exclusion File. NY Medicaid relies completely on the use of
340B claim level identifiers to avoid duplicate discounts. These identifiers are required at the claim submission level for
all 340B drug claims, thereby avoiding duplicate discounts. Using these identifiers is the only way NY Medicaid will
remove the claim from rebate invoicing.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
6.0 Definitions
The following terms are defined for the purposes of the NY Medicaid program and are included to help clarify policies
as provided in this Manual:
340B Ceiling Price
The 340B ceiling price is statutorily defined as the Average Manufacturer Price (AMP) reduced by the rebate
percentage, which is commonly referred to as the Unit Rebate Amount (URA). HRSA obtains the AMP and URA data
from the Centers for Medicare and Medicaid Services (CMS) as part of quarterly reporting for the Medicaid Drug
Rebate Program. This figure is then multiplied by the package size and case package size to produce a price that is
used in the marketplace for purchasing covered outpatient drugs. For example, the AMP minus the URA indicates the
cost of one pill.
Actual Acquisition Cost
Refers to the price paid for a drug or supply by the provider or in the case of a Covered Agency for a 340B purchased
drug, from a supplier with no added costs, delivery fees or dispensing fees, or means the agency's determination of the
pharmacy providers' actual prices paid to acquire drug products marketed or sold by specific manufacturers.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
Note: applying claim level identifiers on non-340B purchased drug claims is
considered fraudulent billing.
The following fields are required on Medicaid 340B drug claims submitted in the NCPDP format:
Medicaid Secondary Claim
Field Medicaid Primary Claim (Medicare. Commercial)
420-DK, Submission Clarification Code (SCC) 20 20
423-DN, Basis of Cost Determination (BCD) 08 N/A
409-D9 Ingredient Cost Submitted 340B Acquisition Cost N/A
Lowest Net Charge to Cash Lowest Net Charge to Cash
426-DQ Usual and Customary Cost (U&C) Customers Customers
FAQs on the 340B program itself, as well as information on how to ask additional questions, can be found on the HRSA
website at: https://www.hrsa.gov/opa/faqs/index.html.

answers.headline_summary: 340B drugs are reimbursed based on their acquisition cost. Claims for these drugs must include specific identifiers and the 340B acquisition cost to avoid duplicate discounts and ensure proper reimbursement.

aligned_pairs.provider_manual: For 340B purchased drugs, reimbursement is based on the 340B Acquisition Cost. A professional dispensing fee of $10.18 is also applied, unless the pharmacys Usual and Customary (U&C) pricing is lower.
aligned_pairs.policy_update: 340B drug claims submitted via NCPDP D.0 format must be submitted at the 340B acquisition cost by invoice to the provider for Fee-For-Service (FFS) members. This cost should be net of any manufacturer discounts or other price reductions.

aligned_pairs.provider_manual: Federal law prohibits duplicate discounts for 340B drugs. New York Medicaid relies on 340B claim level identifiers to avoid these duplicate discounts. Using these identifiers is the only way New York Medicaid will remove the claim from rebate invoicing.
aligned_pairs.policy_update: 340B drug claims submitted to Medicaid via NCPDP D.0 format must be properly identified as 340B for both Fee-For-Service (FFS) and Medicaid Managed Care (MMC) members. This identification is crucial to prevent duplicate discounts.

aligned_pairs.provider_manual: Medicaid 340B drug claims submitted in NCPDP format require specific fields. These include Submission Clarification Code (SCC) 20, Basis of Cost Determination (BCD) 08, Ingredient Cost Submitted as 340B Acquisition Cost, and Usual and Customary Cost (U&C) as the Lowest Net Charge to Cash Customers.
aligned_pairs.policy_update: Drugs obtained via the 340B program must be identified by appending the UD modifier to the 340B drug line on submitted New York State Medicaid Fee-For-Service-only claims. Providers are also required to report the National Drug Code (NDC) and the actual acquisition cost by invoice in the charges field of a claim.

similarities: Both the provider manual and policy update state that reimbursement for 340B drugs is based on the 340B acquisition cost [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines, New York State Medicaid Update August 2019 Volume 35 Number 9].

Both documents emphasize the necessity of identifying 340B claims to prevent duplicate discounts [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines, New York State Medicaid Update August 2019 Volume 35 Number 9].

Both sources require specific identifiers and the reporting of acquisition cost for 340B drug claims [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines, New York State Medicaid Update July 2022 Volume 38 Number 8].
differences: The provider manual specifies a professional dispensing fee of $10.18 for 340B drugs, which is not explicitly mentioned in the policy update [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines].

The provider manual details specific NCPDP fields like Submission Clarification Code (SCC) 20 and Basis of Cost Determination (BCD) 08 for 340B claims, while the policy update mentions appending the UD modifier and reporting the National Drug Code (NDC) [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines, New York State Medicaid Update July 2022 Volume 38 Number 8].

The policy update from July 2022 specifically mentions the UD modifier for Fee-For-Service-only claims and notes different modifiers for Medicare/NYS Medicaid crossover claims, which is not present in the provider manual chunk [New York State Medicaid Update July 2022 Volume 38 Number 8].

policy.text:
National Drug Code Reporting Requirements
Providers are required to report the National Drug Code (NDC) for all PADs billed by a private practitioner to
NYS Medicaid and for all ordered ambulatory claims billed by clinics. If the NDC is not referenced on a claim,
NYS Medicaid will not provide reimbursement for the drug.
Drugs obtained via the 340B program must be identified by appending the UD modifier to the 340B drug line
on submitted NYS Medicaid FFS-only claims. Providers are required to report the NDC on FFS claims for the
reimbursement of 340B drugs. Providers must also include the number of units administered and their actual
acquisition cost, by invoice, in the charges field of a claim. Please note: Medicare requires either the JG or
TB modifiers be appended to the 340B drug line for Medicare/NYS Medicaid crossover claims (no UD modifier
is needed on crossover claims).
An accurate NDC must be reported for all PADs billed to NYS Medicaid FFS on an institutional claim that
uses Ambulatory Patient Groups (APGs) payment methodology.

Federal law prohibits manufacturers from providing both a discounted 340B price and a Medicaid drug rebate for the same drug. These identifiers are the only way NY Medicaid will remove the claim from rebate invoicing.

Providers are required to report the National Drug Code (NDC) on FFS claims for the reimbursement of 340B drugs. Providers must also include the number of units administered and their actual acquisition cost, by invoice, in the charges field of a claim.

These fields include Submission Clarification Code (SCC) 20 and Basis of Cost Determination (BCD) 08 for primary claims. The Ingredient Cost Submitted must be the 340B Acquisition Cost, and the Usual and Customary Cost (U&C) must be the Lowest Net Charge to Cash Customers.

similarities: Both the provider manual and policy state that 340B drugs are reimbursed based on their acquisition cost [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines, New York State Medicaid Update August 2019 Volume 35 Number 9].

Both sources emphasize the necessity of specific identifiers on 340B claims to ensure proper reimbursement and prevent duplicate discounts [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines, New York State Medicaid Update July 2022 Volume 38 Number 8].

The policy highlights the need to report the National Drug Code (NDC) and the number of units administered for 340B drugs, a detail not present in the provider manuals reimbursement methodology section [New York State Medicaid Update July 2022 Volume 38 Number 8].

The provider manual specifies a $10.18 professional dispensing fee in addition to the 340B Acquisition Cost, which is not mentioned in the policys description of 340B reimbursement [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines].

aligned_pairs.policy_update: For 340B drugs, providers must report the National Drug Code (NDC) and append the UD modifier to the drug line on Fee-For-Service (FFS)-only claims. Providers must also include the number of units administered and their actual acquisition cost, by invoice, in the charges field for reimbursement.

aligned_pairs.provider_manual: Federal law prohibits duplicate discounts for 340B drugs. New York Medicaid relies on claim level identifiers to avoid duplicate discounts, and using these identifiers is the only way claims will be removed from rebate invoicing.
aligned_pairs.policy_update: Drugs obtained via the 340B program must be identified by appending the UD modifier to the 340B drug line on submitted New York State Medicaid Fee-For-Service (FFS)-only claims. This identification ensures proper reimbursement and implicitly helps avoid issues like duplicate discounts.

aligned_pairs.provider_manual: Medicaid 340B drug claims submitted in NCPDP format require specific fields. These include Submission Clarification Code (SCC) 20, Basis of Cost Determination (BCD) 08, Ingredient Cost Submitted as 340B Acquisition Cost, and Usual and Customary Cost (U&C) as Lowest Net Charge to Cash Customers.
aligned_pairs.policy_update: 340B drug claims submitted via NCPDP D.0 format must be identified as 340B and submitted at the 340B acquisition cost by invoice. Required fields include SCC 20, BCD 08, Ingredient Cost Submitted as 340B Acquisition Cost, and U&C as Lowest Net Charge to Cash Customers.

similarities: Both the provider manual and policy update specify that 340B drugs are reimbursed based on their acquisition cost [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines, New York State Medicaid Update July 2022 Volume 38 Number 8].

Both documents emphasize the importance of specific claim identifiers to prevent duplicate discounts for 340B drugs [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines, New York State Medicaid Update July 2022 Volume 38 Number 8].

Both sources list the same required fields for 340B drug claims submitted in NCPDP format, such as SCC 20, BCD 08, Ingredient Cost Submitted as 340B Acquisition Cost, and U&C as Lowest Net Charge to Cash Customers [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines, New York State Medicaid Update August 2019 Volume 35 Number 9].
differences: The provider manual explicitly states a professional dispensing fee of $10.18 for 340B purchased drugs, which is not detailed in the policy update regarding reimbursement methodology [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines].

The policy update specifically mentions appending the UD modifier to the 340B drug line on Fee-For-Service (FFS)-only claims for identification, while the provider manual refers more broadly to claim level identifiers [New York State Medicaid Update July 2022 Volume 38 Number 8, New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines].

test_id_51.query_content: How are home infusion drugs covered, and who is responsible for billing
test_id_51.concept: home infusion drugs coverage and billing responsibilities

policy.text:
Reminders:
• It is the responsibility of the providers, both the facility and the pharmacy, to actively and regularly
seek TPL enrollment and Medicaid eligibility for their patients.
• It is the pharmacy’s responsibility to bill timely and to the appropriate party, i.e., the facility, commercial
plan, Medicare, or Medicaid. Information regarding timely billing can be found at: https://www.emedny
.org/ProviderManuals/AllProviders/Guide_to_Timely_Billing.pdf.
• All pharmacies that are not required to bill the facility (pharmacy benefit not included in the rate) must
submit their transactions through the online Prospective Drug Utilization Review (Pro-DUR) program
(https://www.emedny.org/ProviderManuals/Pharmacy/ProDUR-ECCA_Provider_Manual/index.aspx)
using the National Council for Prescription Drug Programs (NCPDP) transaction format. NCPDP
format specifications can be found in the ProDUR ECCA Standards Manual at:
https://www.emedny.org/HIPAA/5010/transactions/NCPDP_D.0_Companion_Guide.pdf.
policy.title: New York State Medicaid Update July 2020 Volume 36 Number 12
policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

policy.text:
NYS Medicaid FFS policy for drugs administered by subcutaneous, intramuscular, or intravenous methods in
the practitioner’s office are covered for Food and Drug Administration (FDA) approved indications, or where
the medically accepted indication is supported in any of the compendia described in Social Security Act
§1927(g)(1)(B)(i) for a medically necessary NYS Medicaid-covered service. In the absence of such a
recognized indication, an approved Institutional Review Board (IRB) protocol would be required with
documentation maintained in the patient's clinical file. Drugs are not covered for investigational or
experimental use.
The NYS Medicaid program has also issued policies and billing guidance for certain drugs/drug classes.
These drugs are eligible for reimbursement when the clinical criteria outlined on the NYS Department of
Health (DOH) New York State Medicaid Fee-for-Service Practitioner Administered Drug Policies and Billing
Guidance web page, located at https://www.health.ny.gov/health_care/medicaid/program/practitioner_
administered/ffs_practitioner_administer.htm, and listed web page Medicaid Update articles, are met.
policy.title: New York State Medicaid Update July 2022 Volume 38 Number 8
policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

policy.text:
Pharmacies will only be able to bill for Medicaid non-dual eligible members. Dual eligible members will
continue to access immunization services through Medicare. Medicaid Managed Care (MMC) enrollees will
continue to access immunization services through their health plans. For Medicaid Managed Care
Organization (MCO) billing guidance, providers must contact the individual plan. Providers can access
individual plan information via the NYS MMC Pharmacy Benefit Information Center web page at:
https://mmcdruginformation.nysdoh.suny.edu/, then select the individual plan and choose “Pharmacy
Vaccine Billing Guidance”.
Reimbursement for these vaccines may be based on a patient specific, or non-patient specific, order. These
orders must be kept on file at the pharmacy. The ordering prescriber must be actively enrolled as a NYS
Medicaid provider, unless otherwise exempt, and the prescribers National Provider Identifier (NPI) is required
on the claim for the claim to be paid.
policy.title: New York State Medicaid Update August 2021 Volume 37 Number 10
policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

policy.text:
Per New York State Medicaid policy, drugs administered by subcutaneous, intramuscular, or intravenous
methods are covered when provided for Federal Drug Administration (FDA)-approved indications and those
recognized off-label indications listed in the drug compendia. Additional information regarding coverage criteria
and billing guidelines for LUXTURNA™ are outlined in the March 2018 issue of the Medicaid Update.
Questions
• Medicaid fee-for-service (FFS) coverage and policy questions may be directed to the Office of Health
Insurance Programs (OHIP) Division of Program Development and Management at (518) 473-2160.
• Medicaid Managed Care (MMC) general coverage questions may be directed to OHIP Division of Health
Plan Contracting and Oversight at covques@health.ny.gov or (518) 473-1134.
• MMC reimbursement and/or billing requirement questions should be directed to the enrollee's MMC plan.
• FFS claim questions should be directed to the eMedNY call center at (800) 343–9000.
policy.title: New York State Medicaid Update February 2019 Volume 35 Number 3
policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
Home Infusion
The New York State Medicaid Program does not provide a bundled payment to cover drugs, supplies and services
associated with home infusion treatments. Home infusion drugs and supplies must be billed as a pharmacy or medical
benefit.
The list of Medicaid reimbursable drugs available as a pharmacy benefit may be accessed at:
https://www.emedny.org/info/formfile.aspx.
The Centers for Medicare and Medicaid Services (CMS) requires coverage of home infusion drugs under Medicare Part
D that are not currently covered under Parts A and B of Medicare. Information on Medicare coverage of home
infusion can be found here: https://www.cms.gov/Medicare/Medicare-Fee-for-Service-Payment/Home-Infusion-
Therapy/Overview.html.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
Pharmacy Dispensing of Drugs That Require Administration by a Practitioner
NYS Medicaid recognizes the need for certain drugs, including home infusion drugs, requiring administration by a
health care provider to be available to members by way of both the medical benefit and pharmacy benefit. Drugs are
evaluated to determine if they are eligible for coverage under the pharmacy benefit using the following criteria:
• The drug must be able to be billed in the NCPDP format.
• The drug must be able to be delivered directly to the place of administration. (See Delivery section)
• The drug must not be included in the rate for a facility.
• The drug must be available from a dispensing pharmacy that is enrolled in Medicaid.
• Providers must ensure that a pharmacist counsel the patient consistent with federal (including but not
limited to 42 CFR § 456.705(c) and SSA § 1927 (g)(2)(A)(ii)) and state law, rule, or regulation (8 NYCRR §
63.6(8)), and retain records for six years of provision of counseling in compliance with federal and state law,
rule, or regulation.
• In certain circumstances, the DUR Board may need to review the drug before clinical criteria may be
applied.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
Providers can refer to the correct billing guidance for these products in the
Medical Supplies Procedure Codes & Coverage Guidelines found here:
https://www.emedny.org/ProviderManuals/DME/PDFS/MedicalSupply_Procedure_Codes.pdf
Parenteral
Examples of non-acceptable parenteral compound claim submissions are those:
• inconsistent with sterile compound criteria as required by State or federal law or regulation (8NYCRR Part 29.2
(13)), or

• made to simply dilute, reconstitute, or otherwise prepare a medication for infusion per it´s labeling, or
• products made for nutrition or hydration. Providers can refer to the correct billing guidance for these products
in the Medical Supplies Procedure Codes & Coverage Guidelines found here:
https://www.emedny.org/ProviderManuals/DME/PDFS/MedicalSupply_Procedure_Codes.pdf
Pharmacy Billing
Per Medicaid policy, when billing a compound via National Council for Prescription Drug Programs (NCPDP) D.0
transaction, providers must submit a minimum of two ingredients, or National Drug Codes (NDCs), in the Compound
Segment, field 489-TE (Compound Product ID). Providers can submit up to 25 NDCs using this field.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
All prescription drugs are reimbursed on a fee-for-service basis. Residential health care
facilities may:
• Operate an institutional pharmacy to provide these items or
• Contract with Medicaid enrolled pharmacies to provide these items to Medicaid members. The pharmacy
must be reimbursed by the facility for all non-prescription drugs and medical/surgical supplies. Prescription
drugs may be billed directly to Medicaid by the dispensing pharmacy on a fee-for-service basis.
Drugs billed directly to Medicaid are limited to prescription drugs included on the New York State Medicaid List of
Reimbursable Drugs: https://www.emedny.org/info/formfile.aspx and are subject to refill, frequency, quantity and
duration, step therapy, and prior authorization/approval requirements as described in this Manual.

Medicaid members with both Medicare and Medicaid (dual eligible Medicaid members) who have met their residency
requirements in a residential health care facility will receive their prescription drug coverage from their Medicare Part D
Plan.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

answers.headline_summary: Home infusion drugs are not covered by a bundled payment in NYS Medicaid but must be billed as a pharmacy or medical benefit. Coverage is provided for FDA-approved indications or medically accepted indications supported by compendia, with specific billing guidance for certain drugs.

aligned_pairs.provider_manual: The New York State Medicaid Program does not offer a bundled payment for home infusion treatments. Instead, home infusion drugs and supplies must be billed as either a pharmacy or medical benefit.
aligned_pairs.policy_update: NYS Medicaid Fee-for-Service policy covers drugs administered by subcutaneous, intramuscular, or intravenous methods in a practitioners office. These drugs are covered for FDA-approved indications or medically accepted indications supported by compendia.

aligned_pairs.provider_manual: Drugs eligible for pharmacy benefit coverage, including home infusion drugs, must be billable in the NCPDP format. They must also be deliverable to the place of administration and not included in a facilitys rate.
aligned_pairs.policy_update: Pharmacies that are not required to bill a facility must submit transactions through the Pro-DUR program using the NCPDP transaction format. It is the pharmacys responsibility to bill the appropriate party, such as the facility, commercial plan, Medicare, or Medicaid, in a timely manner.

similarities: Both the provider manual and policy indicate that drugs requiring administration are covered under specific conditions, including home infusion drugs [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines, New York State Medicaid Update July 2022 Volume 38 Number 8].

Both sources emphasize the use of the NCPDP transaction format for pharmacy billing [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines, New York State Medicaid Update July 2020 Volume 36 Number 12].
differences: The provider manual specifically addresses home infusion treatments and clarifies that they are not covered by a bundled payment, requiring billing as a pharmacy or medical benefit [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines].

The policy update focuses more broadly on drugs administered in a practitioners office, outlining coverage criteria based on FDA approval or medically accepted indications [New York State Medicaid Update July 2022 Volume 38 Number 8].

The policy update explicitly states the pharmacys responsibility to bill the appropriate party, such as a facility or commercial plan, in a timely manner [New York State Medicaid Update July 2020 Volume 36 Number 12].

limited to 42 CFR § 456.705(c) and SSA § 1927 (g)(2)(A)(ii)) and state law, rule, or regulation (8 NYCRR §
63.6(8)), and retain records for six years of provision of counseling in compliance with federal and state law,
rule, or regulation.
• In certain circumstances, the DUR Board may need to review the drug before clinical criteria may be
applied.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
Providers can refer to the correct billing guidance for these products in the
Medical Supplies Procedure Codes & Coverage Guidelines found here:
https://www.emedny.org/ProviderManuals/DME/PDFS/MedicalSupply_Procedure_Codes.pdf
Parenteral
Examples of non-acceptable parenteral compound claim submissions are those:
• inconsistent with sterile compound criteria as required by State or federal law or regulation (8NYCRR Part 29.2
(13)), or

• made to simply dilute, reconstitute, or otherwise prepare a medication for infusion per it´s labeling, or
• products made for nutrition or hydration. Providers can refer to the correct billing guidance for these products
in the Medical Supplies Procedure Codes & Coverage Guidelines found here:
https://www.emedny.org/ProviderManuals/DME/PDFS/MedicalSupply_Procedure_Codes.pdf
Pharmacy Billing
Per Medicaid policy, when billing a compound via National Council for Prescription Drug Programs (NCPDP) D.0
transaction, providers must submit a minimum of two ingredients, or National Drug Codes (NDCs), in the Compound
Segment, field 489-TE (Compound Product ID). Providers can submit up to 25 NDCs using this field.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
All prescription drugs are reimbursed on a fee-for-service basis. Residential health care
facilities may:
• Operate an institutional pharmacy to provide these items or
• Contract with Medicaid enrolled pharmacies to provide these items to Medicaid members. The pharmacy
must be reimbursed by the facility for all non-prescription drugs and medical/surgical supplies. Prescription
drugs may be billed directly to Medicaid by the dispensing pharmacy on a fee-for-service basis.
Drugs billed directly to Medicaid are limited to prescription drugs included on the New York State Medicaid List of
Reimbursable Drugs: https://www.emedny.org/info/formfile.aspx and are subject to refill, frequency, quantity and
duration, step therapy, and prior authorization/approval requirements as described in this Manual.

Medicaid members with both Medicare and Medicaid (dual eligible Medicaid members) who have met their residency
requirements in a residential health care facility will receive their prescription drug coverage from their Medicare Part D
Plan.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

answers.headline_summary: Home infusion drugs are not covered by a bundled payment in NYS Medicaid but must be billed as a pharmacy or medical benefit. Coverage is provided for FDA-approved indications or medically accepted indications supported by compendia, with specific billing guidance for certain drugs.

aligned_pairs.provider_manual: The New York State Medicaid Program does not offer a bundled payment for home infusion treatments. Instead, home infusion drugs and supplies must be billed separately as a pharmacy or medical benefit.
aligned_pairs.policy_update: NYS Medicaid Fee-for-Service policy covers drugs administered by subcutaneous, intramuscular, or intravenous methods in a practitioners office. These drugs are covered for FDA-approved indications or medically accepted indications supported by compendia, provided they are for a medically necessary NYS Medicaid-covered service.

aligned_pairs.provider_manual: Drugs requiring administration by a practitioner, including home infusion drugs, can be covered under the pharmacy benefit if specific criteria are met. These criteria include the ability to bill in NCPDP format, direct delivery to the place of administration, and the drug not being included in a facilitys rate.
aligned_pairs.policy_update: Pharmacies that are not required to bill a facility must submit their transactions through the online Prospective Drug Utilization Review (Pro-DUR) program. This submission must utilize the National Council for Prescription Drug Programs (NCPDP) transaction format, and it is the pharmacys responsibility to bill the appropriate party.

similarities: Both the provider manual and policy update acknowledge that drugs requiring administration by a practitioner can be covered under the pharmacy benefit [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines, New York State Medicaid Update July 2020 Volume 36 Number 12].

Both sources indicate that billing for pharmacy-dispensed drugs should adhere to the NCPDP transaction format [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines, New York State Medicaid Update July 2020 Volume 36 Number 12].
differences: The provider manual specifically addresses home infusion treatments and clarifies that they are not covered by a bundled payment, requiring separate billing as a pharmacy or medical benefit [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines].

The policy update provides broader coverage criteria for administered drugs, focusing on FDA-approved or medically accepted indications, without specifically mentioning home infusion as a distinct category [New York State Medicaid Update July 2022 Volume 38 Number 8].

The policy update emphasizes the pharmacys responsibility to bill the appropriate party, such as a facility, commercial plan, Medicare, or Medicaid [New York State Medicaid Update July 2020 Volume 36 Number 12].

test_id_54.query_content: How are home infusion drugs covered, and who is responsible for billing
test_id_54.concept: home infusion drugs coverage and billing responsibilities

policy.text:
Reminders:
• It is the responsibility of the providers, both the facility and the pharmacy, to actively and regularly
seek TPL enrollment and Medicaid eligibility for their patients.
• It is the pharmacy’s responsibility to bill timely and to the appropriate party, i.e., the facility, commercial
plan, Medicare, or Medicaid. Information regarding timely billing can be found at: https://www.emedny
.org/ProviderManuals/AllProviders/Guide_to_Timely_Billing.pdf.
• All pharmacies that are not required to bill the facility (pharmacy benefit not included in the rate) must
submit their transactions through the online Prospective Drug Utilization Review (Pro-DUR) program
(https://www.emedny.org/ProviderManuals/Pharmacy/ProDUR-ECCA_Provider_Manual/index.aspx)
using the National Council for Prescription Drug Programs (NCPDP) transaction format. NCPDP
format specifications can be found in the ProDUR ECCA Standards Manual at:
https://www.emedny.org/HIPAA/5010/transactions/NCPDP_D.0_Companion_Guide.pdf.
policy.title: New York State Medicaid Update July 2020 Volume 36 Number 12
policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

policy.text:
NYS Medicaid FFS policy for drugs administered by subcutaneous, intramuscular, or intravenous methods in
the practitioner’s office are covered for Food and Drug Administration (FDA) approved indications, or where
the medically accepted indication is supported in any of the compendia described in Social Security Act
§1927(g)(1)(B)(i) for a medically necessary NYS Medicaid-covered service. In the absence of such a
recognized indication, an approved Institutional Review Board (IRB) protocol would be required with
documentation maintained in the patient's clinical file. Drugs are not covered for investigational or
experimental use.
The NYS Medicaid program has also issued policies and billing guidance for certain drugs/drug classes.
These drugs are eligible for reimbursement when the clinical criteria outlined on the NYS Department of
Health (DOH) New York State Medicaid Fee-for-Service Practitioner Administered Drug Policies and Billing
Guidance web page, located at https://www.health.ny.gov/health_care/medicaid/program/practitioner_
administered/ffs_practitioner_administer.htm, and listed web page Medicaid Update articles, are met.
policy.title: New York State Medicaid Update July 2022 Volume 38 Number 8
policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

policy.text:
Pharmacies will only be able to bill for Medicaid non-dual eligible members. Dual eligible members will
continue to access immunization services through Medicare. Medicaid Managed Care (MMC) enrollees will
continue to access immunization services through their health plans. For Medicaid Managed Care
Organization (MCO) billing guidance, providers must contact the individual plan. Providers can access
individual plan information via the NYS MMC Pharmacy Benefit Information Center web page at:
https://mmcdruginformation.nysdoh.suny.edu/, then select the individual plan and choose “Pharmacy
Vaccine Billing Guidance”.
Reimbursement for these vaccines may be based on a patient specific, or non-patient specific, order. These
orders must be kept on file at the pharmacy. The ordering prescriber must be actively enrolled as a NYS
Medicaid provider, unless otherwise exempt, and the prescribers National Provider Identifier (NPI) is required
on the claim for the claim to be paid.
policy.title: New York State Medicaid Update August 2021 Volume 37 Number 10
policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

policy.text:
Per New York State Medicaid policy, drugs administered by subcutaneous, intramuscular, or intravenous
methods are covered when provided for Federal Drug Administration (FDA)-approved indications and those
recognized off-label indications listed in the drug compendia. Additional information regarding coverage criteria
and billing guidelines for LUXTURNA™ are outlined in the March 2018 issue of the Medicaid Update.
Questions
• Medicaid fee-for-service (FFS) coverage and policy questions may be directed to the Office of Health
Insurance Programs (OHIP) Division of Program Development and Management at (518) 473-2160.
• Medicaid Managed Care (MMC) general coverage questions may be directed to OHIP Division of Health
Plan Contracting and Oversight at covques@health.ny.gov or (518) 473-1134.
• MMC reimbursement and/or billing requirement questions should be directed to the enrollee's MMC plan.
• FFS claim questions should be directed to the eMedNY call center at (800) 343–9000.
policy.title: New York State Medicaid Update February 2019 Volume 35 Number 3
policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
Home Infusion
The New York State Medicaid Program does not provide a bundled payment to cover drugs, supplies and services
associated with home infusion treatments. Home infusion drugs and supplies must be billed as a pharmacy or medical
benefit.
The list of Medicaid reimbursable drugs available as a pharmacy benefit may be accessed at:
https://www.emedny.org/info/formfile.aspx.
The Centers for Medicare and Medicaid Services (CMS) requires coverage of home infusion drugs under Medicare Part
D that are not currently covered under Parts A and B of Medicare. Information on Medicare coverage of home
infusion can be found here: https://www.cms.gov/Medicare/Medicare-Fee-for-Service-Payment/Home-Infusion-
Therapy/Overview.html.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
Pharmacy Dispensing of Drugs That Require Administration by a Practitioner
NYS Medicaid recognizes the need for certain drugs, including home infusion drugs, requiring administration by a
health care provider to be available to members by way of both the medical benefit and pharmacy benefit. Drugs are
evaluated to determine if they are eligible for coverage under the pharmacy benefit using the following criteria:
• The drug must be able to be billed in the NCPDP format.
• The drug must be able to be delivered directly to the place of administration. (See Delivery section)
• The drug must not be included in the rate for a facility.
• The drug must be available from a dispensing pharmacy that is enrolled in Medicaid.
• Providers must ensure that a pharmacist counsel the patient consistent with federal (including but not
limited to 42 CFR § 456.705(c) and SSA § 1927 (g)(2)(A)(ii)) and state law, rule, or regulation (8 NYCRR §
63.6(8)), and retain records for six years of provision of counseling in compliance with federal and state law,
rule, or regulation.
• In certain circumstances, the DUR Board may need to review the drug before clinical criteria may be
applied.

These orders must be
kept on file at the pharmacy.
• If billing for both the drug and administration, these claims must be submitted separately.
• For the drug, the claim must be submitted using the ordering prescriber’s NPI in the Prescriber ID field. The
pharmacy will be reimbursed for the medication when billed via National Drug Code (NDC) using
reimbursement methodology.

provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
• Refills of compounds must be specifically requested by the patient or patient´s designee before the item is
prepared and submitted for payment.
• Ingredients in a compound claim will only be reimbursed if available on the NYS Medicaid List of Reimbursable
Drugs.
• Ingredients will only be reimbursed if they are compendia-supported for the intended route of administration
and indication.
• Compounds may not contain excluded drugs or be made for NYS Medicaid excluded indications as per the
Social Security Act §1927(d)(2), including, but not limited to drugs to treat weight loss or sexual dysfunction or
for cosmetic purposes.
• Compounds may not be made to bypass the criteria within the NYS Medicaid Fee-for-Service Programs.
• Compounds may not be made with or to replace drug products removed from the marketplace due to safety
reasons.
• Compounds may not be made in therapeutic amounts or combinations not FDA-approved, or compendia-
supported for use.
• Compounds may not be made to add coloring, flavoring, perfumes, or other non-active ingredient additives to
a commercially available product.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
Multiple Source Drugs
Reimbursement is only available for those multiple source drugs contained on the List of Medicaid Reimbursable
Drugs.
For certain brand name prescriptions not in the Brand Less Than Generic Program, to be eligible for reimbursement,
prescribers must certify that the brand name drug is required by:
• writing directly on the face of the prescription “Brand Necessary” or “Brand Medically Necessary” in their own
handwriting in addition to the “DAW”, or
• in the case of electronic prescriptions, the prescriber must insert an electronic direction to clarify “Brand
Medically Necessary” or “Brand Necessary”.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
TCN 082609865432123 was paid on
October 1, 2008. Later, the provider became aware that the patient was covered by other insurance. The other
insurance was billed, and the provider received full payment from that payer. Medicaid must be reimbursed by
submitting a void to the previously paid claim.

provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

answers.headline_summary: Naloxone reimbursement involves separate billing for the drug and its administration. The drug is reimbursed via NDC using a specific methodology, while administration is reimbursed at a current fee using procedure code 96372. Policy documents, however, do not directly address naloxone reimbursement but rather general service authorization and reimbursement for other services.

aligned_pairs.provider_manual: For naloxone, claims for the drug and its administration must be submitted separately. The drug is reimbursed when billed via National Drug Code (NDC) using reimbursement methodology, with the ordering prescriber's NPI. Administration is reimbursed at the current administration fee using procedure code 96372 and the pharmacy's NPI.
aligned_pairs.policy_update: Managed Care Plans (MMCPs) reimburse for initial authorization of 96 units or 24 hours of service for a 60-day period upon notification from the HCBS provider. This policy does not specifically address naloxone reimbursement but rather general service authorization and reimbursement for other services.

differences: The provider manual provides specific instructions for billing and reimbursement of naloxone drug and administration, including the use of NDCs and procedure code 96372 [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines].

The policy document outlines general reimbursement for initial service authorization by Managed Care Plans for Home and Community Based Services, without mentioning naloxone specifically [Children’s Health and Behavioral Health Medicaid System Transformation Children’s Home and Community Based Services Manual].
answers.caveats: The policy document does not directly address naloxone reimbursement, making a direct comparison difficult. The policy information provided is a general example of service authorization and reimbursement within Medicaid, not specific to naloxone.

provider_manual.text:
These orders must be
kept on file at the pharmacy.
• If billing for both the drug and administration, these claims must be submitted separately.
• For the drug, the claim must be submitted using the ordering prescriber’s NPI in the Prescriber ID field. The
pharmacy will be reimbursed for the medication when billed via National Drug Code (NDC) using
reimbursement methodology.
• For the administration, the claim must be submitted using procedure code 96372, and the pharmacy’s NPI
in the Prescriber ID field. A pharmacy will be reimbursed the current administration fee.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
• Refills of compounds must be specifically requested by the patient or patient´s designee before the item is
prepared and submitted for payment.
• Ingredients in a compound claim will only be reimbursed if available on the NYS Medicaid List of Reimbursable
Drugs.
• Ingredients will only be reimbursed if they are compendia-supported for the intended route of administration
and indication.
• Compounds may not contain excluded drugs or be made for NYS Medicaid excluded indications as per the
Social Security Act §1927(d)(2), including, but not limited to drugs to treat weight loss or sexual dysfunction or
for cosmetic purposes.
• Compounds may not be made to bypass the criteria within the NYS Medicaid Fee-for-Service Programs.
• Compounds may not be made with or to replace drug products removed from the marketplace due to safety
reasons.
• Compounds may not be made in therapeutic amounts or combinations not FDA-approved, or compendia-
supported for use.
• Compounds may not be made to add coloring, flavoring, perfumes, or other non-active ingredient additives to
a commercially available product.

aligned_pairs.policy_update: Managed Care Plans (MMCPs) will reimburse for initial authorization of 96 units or a total of 24 hours of service for a period of 60 days. This reimbursement occurs from the time the MMCP receives notification from the Home and Community Based Services (HCBS) provider.

similarities: Both the provider manual and policy document discuss reimbursement for services.
Both documents imply that specific billing procedures are required for reimbursement.
differences: The provider manual provides specific instructions for billing naloxone drug and administration, including NDCs and procedure codes, while the policy document discusses general reimbursement for authorized service units or hours [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines, Children’s Health and Behavioral Health Medicaid System Transformation Children’s Home and Community Based Services Manual].

The provider manual details the roles of the ordering prescriber's NPI and the pharmacy's NPI for naloxone claims, whereas the policy document focuses on MMCP reimbursement for HCBS providers [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines, Children’s Health and Behavioral Health Medicaid System Transformation Children’s Home and Community Based Services Manual].
answers.caveats: The policy document does not specifically address naloxone reimbursement, but rather discusses general reimbursement for services provided by Managed Care Plans (MMCPs) for Home and Community Based Services (HCBS).

differences: The provider manual specifies the billing procedures and reimbursement for naloxone drug and administration, including the use of NDC for the drug and procedure code 96372 for administration [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines].
